title: Quadlet containers cannot be controlled by Docker-compatible stop/start lifecycle tools because stop removes the container
body:
### Feature request description

Quadlet `.container` units currently create containers with auto-remove behavior. As a result, when a Quadlet-managed container is stopped, the container object is deleted instead of remaining in an exited/stopped state.

This is expected behavior for the current Quadlet model, and I understand that persistent application data should normally be stored in volumes.

However, this creates a separate compatibility problem: external tools that use Docker-compatible lifecycle semantics expect `stop` and `start` to operate on the same existing container object.

A concrete example is Sablier, which can stop idle containers and later start them again on demand. With Quadlet-managed containers, the stop operation removes the container, so the later start operation fails because the container no longer exists.

This is not primarily about preserving mutable filesystem state inside the container. It is about allowing external tools to pause/wake a service using Docker-compatible `stop` / `start` semantics.

### Suggest potential solution

Please consider adding a Quadlet option that allows the container object to remain after stop.

Possible names:
```ini
[Container]
AutoRemove=false
```
or
```ini
[Container]
RemoveOnStop=false
```

With this option enabled, Quadlet could preserve the container object after stop, allowing:
```bash
podman stop my-container
podman start my-container
```

### Have you considered any alternatives?

The main alternative I tried was replacing Quadlet `.container` units with plain systemd `.service` files for the services managed by Sablier.

The reason is that Quadlet-generated units always add `--rm` to the `podman run` command. When Sablier stops an idle container through Docker-compatible lifecycle semantics, the container is removed instead of being left in an exited/stopped state. Later, when Sablier tries to start the same container again, `docker start` / Podman Docker API start fails because the container no longer exists.

The current workaround I am testing is to use regular `.service` files instead of Quadlets for Sablier-managed services. These services run containers with:

```bash
podman run --replace -d ...
```
without --rm.

With this approach, when the container is stopped, it remains available in an Exited state, so Sablier can restart it later using Docker-compatible start semantics.

This workaround appears more compatible with Sablier, but it means giving up the benefits of Quadlet for those services, such as declarative container configuration, simpler unit generation, and cleaner Podman/systemd integration.

### Additional context

#### Related prior discussion

There is an existing old discussion about Quadlet persistence:

- https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/19620

That discussion mainly focused on persistent container state, podmansh, “pet containers”, package installations inside containers, and whether container filesystem changes should survive restarts.

This request is slightly different:

- I am not asking for application data to be stored inside the container.

- I am not asking Quadlet to encourage mutable container filesystems.

- I am asking for a supported mode where a Quadlet-defined container can remain as a stopped container object after stop, so Docker-compatible lifecycle tools can later start it again.

#### Current behavior

A Quadlet `.container` unit generates a `podman run` command with auto-remove behavior.

When the container is stopped, the container is deleted.

This breaks tools that expect:

```bash
docker stop my-container
docker start my-container
```
or equivalent calls through Podman’s Docker-compatible API.

comments.body:
That is simply not possible, either you properly use quadlets or you don't. Using the systemd units is not just a one time job to create the container. Instead systemd does cgroup management, run ExecStartPre/Post=  other units directives that podman does not know about, properly monitors/displays the unit status. If you do a podman start on a container created by quadlet/systemd you have none of that as containers are started as child processes.

So either you teach your tooling to use the proper systemctl start/stop commands for the service or just do not use quadlets and manage the containers the way you did in docker.

Keeping the container and then starting outside the units would definitely defeat the purpose of quadlet so we will not support that.
